policy_statement: Ensure seamless connectivity within the trail system and between on- and off-street trails.
mechanism_description: Improve connectivity between on- and off-street trails to make walking and cycling easier, supporting a shift from car trips to active modes and lowering transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport infrastructure connectivity → modal shift (car → active modes) → ↓ VMT and fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Cities expand and better connect on-street bikeways, sidewalks, and off-street trails into a continuous, usable network.  
(2) Process: Higher connectivity lowers “last-gap” barriers, reduces travel time/effort and perceived safety risk for active travel, increasing the share of trips made by walking/cycling and reducing car trip frequency and distance.  
(3) Outcome: Reduced gasoline/diesel combustion from lower VMT leads to lower CO₂ (and co-pollutant) emissions from the transport sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions via modal shift from car trips to walking/cycling (and reduced VMT).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Infrastructure network connectivity enables modal shift away from private vehicles, reducing fuel combustion and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be co-benefits (health, safety, equity, some heat resilience), the mechanism’s direct climate lever is emissions reduction from reduced car use—i.e., mitigation rather than adaptation or resource efficiency as the primary frame.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a connectivity project is framed mainly for recreation with negligible displacement of car travel, mitigation impact could be smaller; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly includes modal shift and emissions reduction, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is “active network connectivity → modal shift → emissions reduction” is primarily mitigation because its largest climate effect is lowering transport GHGs.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., “active mobility,” “VMT reduction,” “health co-benefits,” “equity access”) depends on policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Connectivity upgrades can also improve evacuation/route redundancy and reduce heat exposure by enabling shorter trips, but these are typically secondary; if projects are purely recreational and do not shift travel from cars, mitigation effects may be limited despite the stated mechanism.
key_indicators: “Strategy”; “Ensure seamless connectivity”; “within the trail system”; “between on and off-street trails”
co_benefits: Improved public health (physical activity), reduced local air pollution, traffic safety, accessibility/mobility equity, recreation benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as infrastructure-investment if accompanied by explicit capital actions/funding to build connectors, but the provided text is a general connectivity strategy without an investment commitment or enforceable standard.
